textPlain: I've used /dev/null for exactly this purpose. I have output that needs to go somewhere, and I don't want to worry about whether that somewhere can handle it.Later on in deployment, it will go somewhere else. Somewhere that has been evaluated for being able to handle it.In that way, /dev/null is to storage what `true` is to execution - it just works.
Always instantly consistent, always available, and perfectly tolerant of partitioning.Truly, it is the only database which can be scaled to unlimited nodes and remain fully CAP.
